About

Travelers’ Choice Best of the Best

2026

Shorthand for Paris—and romance itself—the Eiffel Tower is 1,083 feet tall but looms way larger. All over the city, you’ll spot it on postcards, keychains, even stenciled on cappuccino foam. But it’s worth seeing the real thing up close to fully understand its size and grandeur.

The Esplanade grounds are free to explore, and seeing the latticework in the underbelly of the Tower doesn’t cost a dime. That said, super-fans can shell out for tickets and make the vertical climb upward, passing the first and second floors en route to the very top, which has 360-degree views. Along the way, you’ll pass exhibitions about the Eiffel Tower’s history, plus restaurants, gift shops, and even a spot for a Champagne break—this is France, after all.

## Must-see highlights

- The Summit









The main event: Here at the top, you’ll get a soaring aerial view of Paris.

- Sparkle lights









What’s better than the Eiffel Tower? One lit up in 20,000 shimmering bulbs.

- 2nd Floor









In addition to killer views, there’s a Michelin-starred restaurant and a macaron shop.

- 1st Floor









The views aren’t quite as dramatic here, but there’s a glass floor for fun photo ops.

- The Esplanade









Get another perspective on the Tower by looking up at the ironwork from below.


Things to know

* * *

Know before you go

- Buy tickets online: You’ll get guaranteed access and be able to skip the massive line onsite.
- Procrastinators can still buy same-day tickets online (while supplies last) within a 30-minute window of arrival.
- Tickets vary depending on how high you want to go and whether you want to climb the stairs, take the elevator, or a combo.
- Tickets are time-stamped—give yourself an extra 15 minutes to get through security.
- Reservations are a must at Madame Brasserie and Le Jules Verne, the on-site restaurants. If you didn’t book a table, you can still hit the buffets and Champagne Bar.
- The Tower’s sparkling lights start at dusk and go every hour on the hour for five minutes at a time.
- The Champ de Mars greenspace in front of the Eiffel Tower is an excellent spot for a park-bench picnic.
- For the best photo ops with the Tower as a backdrop, head to the Esplanade de Trocadéro.

The Eiffel Tower, a must-see for many travelers, offers unforgettable views that justify its cost. Despite heavy crowds and high prices, the iconic atmosphere, especially at sunset when it sparkles, remains awe-inspiring. Easily reachable via public transport, visitors often spend 1-3 hours exploring.

Reviews highlight booking tickets online in advance to avoid long queues. While staff experiences vary, the tower is praised for being accessible to all ages. Some travelers recommend visiting early or at night for a more magical experience.

About
Travelers’ Choice Best of the Best
2026
Shorthand for Paris—and romance itself—the Eiffel Tower is 1,083 feet tall but looms way larger. All over the city, you’ll spot it on postcards, keychains, even stenciled on cappuccino foam. But it’s worth seeing the real thing up close to fully understand its size and grandeur. The Esplanade grounds are free to explore, and seeing the latticework in the underbelly of the Tower doesn’t cost a dime. That said, super-fans can shell out for tickets and make the vertical climb upward, passing the first and second floors en route to the very top, which has 360-degree views. Along the way, you’ll pass exhibitions about the Eiffel Tower’s history, plus restaurants, gift shops, and even a spot for a Champagne break—this is France, after all.
Duration: 1-2 hours

Must-see highlights

  • The Summit
    The main event: Here at the top, you’ll get a soaring aerial view of Paris.
  • Sparkle lights
    What’s better than the Eiffel Tower? One lit up in 20,000 shimmering bulbs.
  • 2nd Floor
    In addition to killer views, there’s a Michelin-starred restaurant and a macaron shop.
  • 1st Floor
    The views aren’t quite as dramatic here, but there’s a glass floor for fun photo ops.
  • The Esplanade
    Get another perspective on the Tower by looking up at the ironwork from below.
Things to know
  • Buy tickets online: You’ll get guaranteed access and be able to skip the massive line onsite.
  • Procrastinators can still buy same-day tickets online (while supplies last) within a 30-minute window of arrival.
  • Tickets vary depending on how high you want to go and whether you want to climb the stairs, take the elevator, or a combo.
  • Tickets are time-stamped—give yourself an extra 15 minutes to get through security.
  • Reservations are a must at Madame Brasserie and Le Jules Verne, the on-site restaurants. If you didn’t book a table, you can still hit the buffets and Champagne Bar.
  • The Tower’s sparkling lights start at dusk and go every hour on the hour for five minutes at a time.
  • The Champ de Mars greenspace in front of the Eiffel Tower is an excellent spot for a park-bench picnic.
  • For the best photo ops with the Tower as a backdrop, head to the Esplanade de Trocadéro.
Completed in 1889, this colossal landmark, although initially hated by many Parisians, is now a famous symbol of French civic pride.

The Eiffel Tower, a must-see for many travelers, offers unforgettable views that justify its cost. Despite heavy crowds and high prices, the iconic atmosphere, especially at sunset when it sparkles, remains awe-inspiring. Easily reachable via public transport, visitors often spend 1-3 hours exploring. Reviews highlight booking tickets online in advance to avoid long queues. While staff experiences vary, the tower is praised for being accessible to all ages. Some travelers recommend visiting early or at night for a more magical experience.
